_doc: One verifiably-FALSE sibling per canonical theorem. Each is a single meaning-changing edit of the TRUE statement with a concrete counterexample. Used to build a 50/50 balanced TRUE/FALSE eval that defuses Reviewer Y7JT's all-TRUE confound. numeric counterexamples are machine-checked by verify_false_siblings.py.

siblings.false_stmt: Every prime number is at least 3
Every prime number is greater than 2
The number 1 is prime
siblings.counterexample: primes are > 1 by definition, so 1 is not prime

siblings.false_stmt: 4 is a prime number
If two natural numbers each divide the other, then both are zero
siblings.counterexample: 2 divides 2 and 2 divides 2, but 2 is not 0

siblings.false_stmt: Every natural number divides its successor
Zero divides every natural number
Zero divides every natural number
A number divides the sum of two numbers if and only if it divides each of them
If a number divides a product, it divides at least one of the factors
For integers a and b, a = b * (a div b) - (a mod b)
Any natural number modulo itself is one
Any natural number modulo one equals itself
The square of any real number is strictly positive
siblings.counterexample: 0 squared is 0, which is not strictly positive

siblings.false_stmt: Subtraction is commutative
Division is commutative
Subtraction is associative
Division is associative
Adding one to any element returns that element
One plus any element is that element
Any element multiplied by zero is itself
Zero times any element is that element
The square of the absolute value equals the number itself
The absolute value of any real number is strictly positive
The degree of a sum of polynomials always equals the maximum of their degrees
siblings.counterexample: x + (-x) = 0; degree of 0 is not equal to max degree 1

siblings.false_stmt: The degree of a product of polynomials equals the product of their degrees
siblings.counterexample: deg(x * x)=2 but product of degrees 1*1=1

siblings.false_stmt: Every set is a proper subset of itself
siblings.counterexample: a set is never a proper subset of itself

siblings.false_stmt: The subset relation is symmetric
siblings.counterexample: empty set is a subset of {1} but {1} is not a subset of the empty set

siblings.false_stmt: Set difference is commutative
The union of two sets always equals the first set
siblings.counterexample: empty union {1} = {1}, not the empty set

siblings.false_stmt: Set difference is associative
Set difference is associative
Every set is a subset of the empty set
siblings.counterexample: {1} is not a subset of the empty set

siblings.false_stmt: The intersection of any set with the empty set is that set
The union of any set with the empty set is the empty set
The cardinality of the empty finite set is one
A singleton finite set has cardinality 2
The factorial of any natural number is greater than that number
Binomial coefficients satisfy C(n, k) = C(k, n)
C(n, 0) = 0 for all natural numbers n
C(n, n) = n for all natural numbers n
The real square root function is always strictly positive
siblings.counterexample: sqrt(0) = 0, which is not strictly positive

siblings.false_stmt: For every real number x, the square root of x squared is x
For every real number x, sqrt(x) * sqrt(x) = x
siblings.counterexample: x=-1: sqrt(-1) is not real / equals 0 in Lean, so the product is not -1

siblings.false_stmt: In a group, every element times its inverse equals that element
siblings.counterexample: that would force every inverse to be the identity; false in any nontrivial group

siblings.false_stmt: In a group, the inverse of an element times the element equals that inverse
siblings.counterexample: that would force every element to be the identity; false in any nontrivial group

siblings.false_stmt: The inverse of the inverse of an element equals its inverse
siblings.counterexample: that would force every element to equal its own inverse; false in any nontrivial group

siblings.false_stmt: In any monoid, if a*b = a*c then b = c
siblings.counterexample: in the natural numbers under multiplication, 0*1 = 0*2 but 1 is not 2

siblings.false_stmt: Every element is strictly less than itself
The less-than-or-equal relation is symmetric
